content:
# Leviticus Chapter 23 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 71v–72r
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 109–110
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_023.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 23 is the sacred-calendar chapter of the Holiness Code — the most comprehensive festival legislation in the Torah, and the structural heart of Israel's temporal life with the LORD. The governing concept is the *mo'ed* (plural: *mo'adim*) — the "appointed time/meeting," from the root *y-'-d* ("to appoint, to meet by appointment"). These are not merely Israel's annual holidays but YHWH's own scheduled appointments with his people: times he has fixed in the calendar for covenant encounter, analogous to the sanctuary as a fixed coordinate of sacred space.

The chapter divides into five divine-speech sections (vv. 1, 9, 23, 26, 33) covering seven appointed times: (1) the weekly Sabbath (v. 3), which functions as the temporal foundation of the whole system; (2) Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread (vv. 4–8); (3) Firstfruits / the omer wave offering, with the counting toward Shavuot (vv. 9–14); (4) Shavuot / the Feast of Weeks / Pentecost (vv. 15–22, including the gleaning law of v. 22); (5) the Day of Trumpet Blast — Rosh Hashanah in rabbinic tradition (vv. 23–25); (6) the Day of Atonements — Yom Kippurim (vv. 26–32); and (7) the Feast of Booths / Sukkot (vv. 33–43), with a closing summary (v. 44).

Four recurring structural markers appear at each appointed time: (*a*) a temporal specification by date and month; (*b*) *miqra-qodesh* — "a holy convocation"; (*c*) prohibition of *melaket avodah* — "laborious/ordinary work"; and (*d*) *isheh la-YHWH* — "a fire-offering / food offering to the LORD." The Sabbath and the Day of Atonement uniquely receive the intensified designation *shabbat shabbaton* — "a sabbath of solemn rest." The Day of Atonement adds a fifth element unique in the calendar: *inui nefesh* — the command to "afflict yourselves," the bodily self-denial that constitutes the fast.

The chapter's closing verse (v. 44) is the only verse in Leviticus where Moses — not YHWH — is the grammatical subject of *dibber* ("spoke/declared"): "Moses declared to the sons of Israel the appointed times of the LORD." This marks the formal promulgation of the calendar to the community. The calendar is not a human cultural product; it is a divinely ordained temporal architecture that Moses hands on.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | *bney yisrael* = "sons of Israel" | *mo'ed* = "appointed time" | *miqra qodesh* = "holy convocation" | *shabbat shabbaton* = "sabbath of solemn rest" | *melaket avodah* = "laborious work" (v. 3 *kol melakhah* = "all work") | *isheh* = "food offering" (following ESV convention) | *ve-innitem* = "you shall afflict yourselves" | *chattat* = "sin offering" | *olah* = "burnt offering" | *shelamim* = "peace offerings" | *ger* = "sojourner" | *atseret* = "solemn assembly" | English name forms throughout

---

## Consensus Translation

**23:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23:2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: The appointed times of the LORD that you shall proclaim as holy convocations — these are my appointed times.

**23:3** Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day is a sabbath of solemn rest, a holy convocation. You shall do no work. It is a sabbath to the LORD in all your dwelling places.

**23:4** "These are the appointed times of the LORD, the holy convocations, which you shall proclaim at the time appointed for them.

**23:5** In the first month, on the fourteenth day of the month at twilight, is the LORD's Passover.

**23:6** And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the Feast of Unleavened Bread to the LORD; for seven days you shall eat unleavened bread.

**23:7** On the first day you shall have a holy convocation; you shall do no laborious work.

**23:8** But you shall present a food offering to the LORD for seven days. On the seventh day is a holy convocation; you shall do no laborious work."

**23:9** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23:10** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land that I give you and you reap its harvest, you shall bring the sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest to the priest,

**23:11** and he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD, so that you may be accepted. On the day after the sabbath the priest shall wave it.

**23:12** And on the day when you wave the sheaf, you shall offer a male lamb a year old without blemish as a burnt offering to the LORD.

**23:13** And the grain offering with it shall be two tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, a food offering to the LORD with a pleasing aroma, and the drink offering with it shall be of wine, a fourth of a hin.

**23:14** And you shall eat neither bread nor roasted grain nor fresh grain until this very day, until you have brought the offering of your God. It is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**23:15** "You shall count for yourselves from the day after the sabbath, from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering. Seven complete weeks shall there be.

**23:16** You shall count fifty days to the day after the seventh week. Then you shall present a grain offering of new grain to the LORD.

**23:17** You shall bring from your dwelling places two loaves of bread to be waved, made of two tenths of an ephah. They shall be of fine flour, and they shall be baked with leaven, as firstfruits to the LORD.

**23:18** And you shall present with the bread seven lambs a year old without blemish, and one bull from the herd and two rams. They shall be a burnt offering to the LORD, with their grain offering and their drink offerings, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**23:19** And you shall offer one male goat for a sin offering, and two male lambs a year old as a sacrifice of peace offerings.

**23:20** And the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering before the LORD, with the two lambs. They shall be holy to the LORD for the priest.

**23:21** And you shall proclaim on this very day. You shall hold a holy convocation. You shall do no laborious work. It is a statute forever in all your dwelling places throughout your generations.

**23:22** "And when you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right to its edge, nor shall you gather the gleanings after your harvest. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner: I am the LORD your God."

**23:23** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23:24** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: In the seventh month, on the first day of the month, you shall have a solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast, a holy convocation.

**23:25** You shall do no laborious work, and you shall present a food offering to the LORD."

**23:26** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23:27** "Now on the tenth day of this seventh month is the Day of Atonements. It shall be for you a holy convocation, and you shall afflict yourselves and present a food offering to the LORD.

**23:28** And you shall do no work on that same day, for it is a Day of Atonements, to make atonement for you before the LORD your God.

**23:29** For any person who is not afflicted on that same day shall be cut off from her people.

**23:30** And any person who does any work on that same day — that person I will destroy from among her people.

**23:31** You shall do no work. It is a statute forever throughout your generations in all your dwelling places.

**23:32** It shall be to you a sabbath of solemn rest, and you shall afflict yourselves. On the ninth day of the month at evening, from evening to evening, you shall keep your sabbath."

**23:33** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**23:34** "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying: On the fifteenth day of this seventh month and for seven days is the Feast of Booths to the LORD.

**23:35** On the first day shall be a holy convocation; you shall do no laborious work.

**23:36** For seven days you shall present food offerings to the LORD. On the eighth day you shall hold a holy convocation and present a food offering to the LORD. It is a solemn assembly; you shall do no laborious work.

**23:37** "These are the appointed times of the LORD, which you shall proclaim as holy convocations, for presenting to the LORD food offerings, burnt offerings and grain offerings, sacrifices and drink offerings, each on its proper day —

**23:38** besides the LORD's sabbaths and besides your gifts and besides all your vow offerings and besides all your freewill offerings, which you give to the LORD.

**23:39** "On the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when you have gathered in the produce of the land, you shall celebrate the feast of the LORD for seven days. On the first day shall be a solemn rest, and on the eighth day shall be a solemn rest.

**23:40** And you shall take on the first day the fruit of splendid trees, branches of palm trees and boughs of leafy trees and willows of the brook, and you shall rejoice before the LORD your God for seven days.

**23:41** You shall celebrate it as a feast to the LORD for seven days in the year. It is a statute forever throughout your generations; you shall celebrate it in the seventh month.

**23:42** You shall dwell in booths for seven days. All native Israelites shall dwell in booths,

**23:43** that your generations may know that I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths when I brought them out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God."

**23:44** Thus Moses declared to the sons of Israel the appointed times of the LORD.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention. EMET's "Moshe" is the Hebrew form, appropriate for EMET's hyper-literal register but not the consensus standard. This same ruling applies at vv. 9, 23, 26, 33, and 44.

**V. 2 — Opening structure.** EMET sets the verse as a command block with bracketed Hebrew glosses. LOGOS and PNEUMA render it in clean prose. PNEUMA: "These are the LORD's appointed times — the sacred assemblies you are to proclaim. These are my appointed times." Consensus: LOGOS's structure — "The appointed times of the LORD that you shall proclaim as holy convocations — these are my appointed times." This preserves the logical sequence: the definition (*mo'adei YHWH*) → the act (*proclaim as holy convocations*) → the possessive claim (*these are my appointed times*). PNEUMA's "sacred assemblies" for *miqra'ei qodesh* is rejected; "holy convocations" is the project ruling.

**V. 2 — "Israelites" vs. "sons of Israel."** PNEUMA uses "Israelites" throughout. The project term is "sons of Israel" (*bney yisrael*) and is applied consistently in the consensus. This ruling applies at every occurrence (vv. 2, 10, 24, 34, 42, 43, 44).

**V. 3 — Work prohibition formula: "work" vs. "laborious work."** All three agents handle v. 3 differently. EMET: "all labor you shall not do [*kol-melakhah*]." LOGOS: "You shall do no work." PNEUMA: "You must not do any regular work on it." The Hebrew of v. 3 uses *kol-melakhah* ("all work") — the absolute, comprehensive Sabbath prohibition — not *melaket avodah* ("laborious/ordinary work"), which is the lesser prohibition used on festival days. Consensus: "You shall do no work" (LOGOS) for v. 3, preserving the distinction. On all festival days (vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36), where the MT has *kol-melaket avodah*, the consensus renders "you shall do no laborious work." This important distinction — total prohibition on the Sabbath, restricted prohibition on festivals — is maintained throughout.

**V. 3 — "Sabbath of solemn rest" for *shabbat shabbaton*.** EMET: "sabbath of complete rest." LOGOS: "sabbath of solemn rest." PNEUMA: "Sabbath of complete rest." Consensus: "sabbath of solemn rest" — LOGOS. Both "solemn rest" and "complete rest" accurately render the intensive doubled construction *shabbat shabbaton*. The project follows LOGOS/ESV's "solemn rest" as the established formal rendering that captures both the comprehensive cessation and the grave character of the day. This applies in vv. 3 and 32.

**V. 3 — "In all your dwelling places."** EMET: "in all your dwellings." LOGOS: "in all your dwelling places." PNEUMA: "wherever you live." Consensus: "in all your dwelling places" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the legal formality of *be-khol moshvoteikhem*. This formula recurs in vv. 14, 17, 21, 31; the consensus applies "in all your dwelling places" consistently.

**V. 4 — "At the time appointed for them."** EMET: "at their appointed times [*be-mo'adam*]." LOGOS: "at the time appointed for them." PNEUMA: "at their appointed seasons." Consensus: "at the time appointed for them" — LOGOS. EMET's "their appointed times" creates a slight redundancy (the verse has already used *mo'adim*); LOGOS's phrasing avoids this. PNEUMA's "seasons" loses the precision of *mo'ed* as a divinely fixed appointment.

**V. 5 — "At twilight" for *bein ha-arbayim*.** EMET: "between the two evenings." LOGOS: "at twilight." PNEUMA: "at twilight." Consensus: "at twilight" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "between the two evenings" is more literal but less natural in English; "at twilight" is the standard formal equivalent (ESV, NASB) and is used consistently.

**V. 7 — "Laborious work" for *melaket avodah*.** EMET: "laborious work." LOGOS: "ordinary work." PNEUMA: "regular work." Consensus: "laborious work" — EMET, following the project's established terminology ruling. The compound *melaket avodah* deserves a compound English rendering that conveys its intensifying force; "laborious work" is more precise than "ordinary work" or "regular work" as a translation of a deliberate technical term. This applies at every festival occurrence (vv. 7, 8, 21, 25, 35, 36).

**V. 8 — "Fire-offering" vs. "food offering" for *isheh*.** EMET: "fire-offering." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "food offering." Consensus: "food offering" — LOGOS/PNEUMA, following ESV convention. The term *isheh* is traditionally understood as "fire-offering" (from *esh*, "fire"), but many modern scholars read it as "food-offering" (from Ugaritic *'th*). The ESV/NASB convention "food offering" is adopted for the consensus throughout, reserving a note on the lexical debate for the disagreement log.

**V. 10 — "When you come into the land that I give you."** EMET: "when you come into the land which I am giving to you." LOGOS: "when you come into the land that I give you." PNEUMA: "when you enter the land I am going to give you." Consensus: "when you come into the land that I give you" — LOGOS. All three are substantively equivalent; LOGOS is cleanest. PNEUMA's "I am going to give" adds a futurist nuance not in the Hebrew present participle *noten lakhem*.

**V. 10 — "Sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest."** EMET: "the first sheaf [*omer reshit*] of your harvest." LOGOS: "the sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest." PNEUMA: "the very first sheaf of your harvest." Consensus: "the sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *omer reshit qetsirkhem* literally means "the *omer* (sheaf-measure) of the beginning/firstfruits of your harvest." LOGOS captures both elements. EMET's "first sheaf" is acceptable but PNEUMA's "the very first sheaf" adds "very" beyond the Hebrew.

**V. 11 — "So that you may be accepted."** EMET: "for your acceptance [*li-rtsoneikhem*]." LOGOS: "so that you may be accepted." PNEUMA: "so it will be accepted on your behalf." Consensus: "so that you may be accepted" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *li-rtsoneikhem* ("for your acceptance / for your favor") describes the purpose of the waving. LOGOS's rendering is the most natural English expression of this purpose. PNEUMA's "it will be accepted" shifts the subject from Israel to the offering.

**V. 11 — "On the day after the sabbath."** All three agents: "on the day after the sabbath" — universal agreement on the literal rendering of *mi-macharat ha-shabbat*. Consensus: "on the day after the sabbath." The contested interpretive question (which "sabbath" is meant — the weekly Sabbath or the first day of Unleavened Bread?) is not adjudicated in the translation; the Hebrew is rendered literally.

**V. 12 — "You shall offer" vs. "you shall prepare."** EMET: "you shall prepare [*ta'asu*]." LOGOS: "you shall offer." PNEUMA: "you must sacrifice." Consensus: "you shall offer" — LOGOS. The verb *ta'asu* is literally "you shall do/make," but in a sacrificial context "offer" is the idiomatic English equivalent. EMET's "prepare" is too weak; PNEUMA's "sacrifice" is accurate but less standard for *'asah* in this context.

**V. 13 — "Fine flour mixed with oil."** EMET: "fine flour [*solet*] mixed with oil." LOGOS: "fine flour mixed with oil." PNEUMA: "the finest flour mixed with oil." Consensus: "fine flour mixed with oil" — LOGOS/EMET. PNEUMA's "finest" adds a superlative not in *solet* (which simply means sifted/refined wheat flour, not "finest").

**V. 13 — "A fourth of a hin."** All three: "a fourth of a hin" — agreement. Consensus: "a fourth of a hin."

**V. 14 — "Until this very day."** EMET: "until this very day [*ad etsem ha-yom ha-zeh*]." LOGOS: "until this same day." PNEUMA: "until the very day." Consensus: "until this very day" — EMET. The Hebrew *etsem ha-yom* ("the bone/substance of the day") is an emphatic idiom best rendered "this very day" rather than LOGOS's flatter "this same day."

**V. 14 — "The offering of your God."** EMET: "the offering of your God [*qorban Eloheikem*]." LOGOS: "the offering of your God." PNEUMA: "this offering to your God." Consensus: "the offering of your God" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "this offering" substitutes a demonstrative for the genitive construct; the Hebrew has a possessive, not a demonstrative.

**V. 15 — "You shall count for yourselves."** EMET: "you shall count for yourselves [*u-sefartem lakhem*]." LOGOS: "you shall count." PNEUMA: "Count off." Consensus: "you shall count for yourselves" — EMET. The Hebrew *lakhem* ("for yourselves") is present and theologically significant: the counting is a personal, active obligation of each household, not merely an official priestly calendar function. LOGOS and PNEUMA both drop *lakhem*.

**V. 15 — "From the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering."** EMET: "from the day you bring the *omer* of the wave offering." LOGOS: "from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering." PNEUMA: "from the day you brought the wave offering of the sheaf." Consensus: "from the day that you brought the sheaf of the wave offering" — LOGOS. "The sheaf" (*ha-omer*) is more informative for English readers than the transliterated "*omer*" (EMET). LOGOS's word order ("sheaf of the wave offering") correctly reflects the Hebrew *omer ha-tenufah*.

**V. 15 — "Seven complete weeks."** EMET and LOGOS: "seven complete weeks." PNEUMA: "seven full weeks." Consensus: "seven complete weeks" — EMET/LOGOS. Both are acceptable; "complete" is the established formal rendering of *temimot* (cf. ESV, NASB).

**V. 16 — "To the day after the seventh week."** EMET: "until the day after the seventh week." LOGOS: "to the day after the seventh week." PNEUMA: "up to the day after the seventh Sabbath." Consensus: "to the day after the seventh week" — LOGOS. The Hebrew *ha-shabbat ha-shevi'it* can be rendered "the seventh week" (following the convention that *shabbatot* = "weeks" in the counting context) or "the seventh Sabbath." Consensus follows LOGOS/EMET's "week" here (the counting is for weeks, not calendar Sabbaths per se). PNEUMA's "Sabbath" is defensible but risks confusing the issue.

**V. 17 — "From your dwelling places."** EMET: "from your dwellings." LOGOS: "from your dwelling places." PNEUMA: "From wherever you live." Consensus: "from your dwelling places" — LOGOS, maintaining the consistent *moshvoteikhem* formula.

**V. 17 — "Two loaves of bread to be waved."** EMET: "two loaves of bread as a wave offering." LOGOS: "two loaves of bread to be waved." PNEUMA: "two loaves of bread made of two-tenths of an ephah of the finest flour." Consensus: "two loaves of bread to be waved" — LOGOS. The phrasing "to be waved" clearly marks the purpose. PNEUMA incorporates the measurement into this clause, creating an overlong rendering; the consensus keeps the measurement in its natural position with the flour specification.

**V. 17 — "Baked with leaven."** EMET: "they shall be baked leavened [*chamets te'afenah*]." LOGOS: "they shall be baked with leaven." PNEUMA: "baked with yeast." Consensus: "they shall be baked with leaven" — LOGOS. "Leaven" (*chamets*) is the traditional and theologically weighted term; "yeast" (PNEUMA) is a modern equivalent but loses the festival connotation of *chamets* as the term used throughout the Unleavened Bread laws.

**V. 18 — "One bull from the herd."** EMET: "one bull of the herd [*par echad ben-bakar*]." LOGOS: "one bull from the herd." PNEUMA: "one young bull." Consensus: "one bull from the herd" — LOGOS/EMET. PNEUMA's "young" translates *ben-bakar* ("son of the herd" = a young bull), but "from the herd" is more standard and parallels similar formulas elsewhere in Leviticus.

**V. 18 — "A food offering with a pleasing aroma."** EMET: "a fire-offering of pleasing aroma." LOGOS: "a food offering with a pleasing aroma." PNEUMA: "a food offering, an aroma pleasing to the LORD." Consensus: "a food offering with a pleasing aroma to the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA combined. The full formula *isheh reah nihoach la-YHWH* includes all three elements; the consensus renders all of them.

**V. 19 — "Peace offerings" for *shelamim*.** EMET: "a peace offering." LOGOS: "a sacrifice of peace offerings." PNEUMA: "a fellowship offering." Consensus: "a sacrifice of peace offerings" — LOGOS. *Zevach shelamim* ("a sacrifice of peace offerings") preserves both nouns. PNEUMA's "fellowship offering" (NIV convention) is an interpretive rendering of *shelamim*; "peace offerings" is the established project term.

**V. 20 — "The priest shall wave them... with the bread of the firstfruits."** EMET: "the priest shall wave them along with the bread of the firstfruits." LOGOS: "the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits as a wave offering before the LORD, with the two lambs." PNEUMA: "The priest is to wave the two lambs before the LORD as a wave offering, together with the bread of the firstfruits." Consensus: LOGOS. The MT sequence — he waves *them* (the lambs) with (*al*) the bread, as a wave offering before the LORD, with (*al*) the two lambs — is complex; LOGOS follows the Hebrew order most closely.

**V. 21 — "And you shall proclaim on this very day."** EMET: "and you shall proclaim on this very day [*u-qratem be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh*]." LOGOS: "And you shall make a proclamation on the same day." PNEUMA: "On that same day you are to proclaim a sacred assembly." Consensus: "And you shall proclaim on this very day" — EMET. The emphatic *etsem ha-yom* ("this very day") is important; LOGOS's "on the same day" softens the emphasis. PNEUMA inserts "a sacred assembly" as the object of the proclamation, which goes beyond the Hebrew (the verb stands alone).

**V. 22 — "Right to its edge" vs. "right up to its edge."** EMET: "you shall not complete the corner of your field." LOGOS: "you shall not reap your field right up to its edge." PNEUMA: "do not reap all the way to the very edges of your field." Consensus: "you shall not reap your field right to its edge" — LOGOS adjusted. The Hebrew *lo tekhaleh pe'at sadkha* is literally "you shall not complete the edge of your field." LOGOS's "right up to its edge" captures this precisely. PNEUMA's "all the way to the very edges" is emphatic but accurate; LOGOS is cleaner.

**V. 22 — "Gleanings after your harvest."** EMET: "the gleaning of your harvest [*leket qetsirekha*]." LOGOS: "the gleanings after your harvest." PNEUMA: "gather the gleanings of your harvest." Consensus: "the gleanings after your harvest" — LOGOS. The *leket* ("fallen/scattered grain left by harvesters") is a technical term; "gleanings" is the established English equivalent.

**V. 22 — "The poor and for the sojourner."** EMET: "for the poor [*la-ani*] and for the sojourner [*ve-la-ger*]." LOGOS: "for the poor and for the sojourner." PNEUMA: "for the poor and the foreigner." Consensus: "for the poor and for the sojourner" — LOGOS/EMET. *Ger* = "sojourner" per project ruling; PNEUMA's "foreigner" is a less precise rendering.

**V. 24 — "Solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast."** EMET: "a rest [*shabbaton*], a memorial of trumpet blast [*zikron teru'ah*], a holy convocation." LOGOS: "a solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast, a holy convocation." PNEUMA: "a day of rest — a sacred assembly signaled by trumpet blasts." Consensus: "a solemn rest, a memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast, a holy convocation" — LOGOS. LOGOS preserves all three elements: *shabbaton* ("solemn rest"), *zikron teru'ah* ("memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast"), and *miqra-qodesh* ("holy convocation"). PNEUMA conflates and rearranges; "sacred assembly" for *miqra-qodesh* is rejected (project ruling: "holy convocation"). EMET's plain "a rest" for *shabbaton* is slightly weaker than "a solemn rest."

**V. 27 — "Day of Atonements" for *Yom Kippurim*.** EMET: "the Day of Atonements [*yom ha-kippurim*]." LOGOS: "the Day of Atonement." PNEUMA: "the Day of Atonement." Consensus: "the Day of Atonements" — EMET. The Hebrew *Yom ha-Kippurim* uses the plural *kippurim* ("atonings"), indicating the comprehensive or intensive character of the day's atonement. The singular "Day of Atonement" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is the common English form, but the plural "Day of Atonements" is more accurate and is preferred for the consensus. Applied consistently in vv. 27 and 28.

**V. 27 — "You shall afflict yourselves."** EMET: "you shall afflict yourselves [*ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem*]." LOGOS: "you shall afflict yourselves." PNEUMA: "humble yourselves." Consensus: "you shall afflict yourselves" — EMET/LOGOS. The project terminology ruling mandates "afflict yourselves" per the task instruction (*ve-innitem* = "you shall afflict yourselves," NOT "humble yourselves"). PNEUMA's "humble yourselves" adopts the LXX (*tapeinōsete*) interpretation at the expense of the Hebrew's more physical sense of bodily self-denial. Applied in vv. 27, 29, and 32.

**V. 28 — "All work" vs. "any work."** EMET: "all labor [*kol-melakhah*]." LOGOS: "no work." PNEUMA: "no work." Consensus: "no work" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT has *kol-melakhah* ("any/all work") for the Day of Atonement (v. 28), the absolute prohibition matching v. 3. The rendering "you shall do no work" (v. 28) follows the pattern established for v. 3 and distinguishes the absolute prohibition from the lesser *melaket avodah* prohibition on festival days.

**V. 29 — "Any person" / pronoun form.** EMET and LOGOS: "whoever / any person." PNEUMA: "anyone who." All use singular third person. The Hebrew *ha-nefesh* is grammatically feminine, so EMET's pronoun "her peoples" (*me-ammeiha*) is technically correct. Consensus: "any person... shall be cut off from her people" — preserving the feminine Hebrew pronoun as in EMET. This is the literal form. LOGOS uses "his people" (normalizing to masculine); PNEUMA uses "their people" (gender-inclusive). The Hebrew *me-ammeiha* is feminine; the consensus preserves "her people" as the literal rendering, consistent with the project's commitment to formal precision. This also applies in v. 30 (*mi-qerev ammah* — "from among her people").

**V. 30 — "That person I will destroy."** EMET: "I will destroy that person [*ve-ha'avadti et-ha-nefesh ha-hi*]." LOGOS: "that person I will destroy." PNEUMA: "I will destroy from among their people anyone who does any work on that day." Consensus: "that person I will destroy from among her people" — EMET/LOGOS combined. The emphatic first-person divine action (*ve-ha'avadti*, "and I will destroy") with the LORD as subject is preserved. The full phrase includes *mi-qerev ammah* ("from among her people"), which LOGOS includes but PNEUMA restructures.

**V. 32 — "At evening" vs. "beginning at evening."** EMET: "on the ninth of the month in the evening [*ba-erev*]." LOGOS: "On the ninth day of the month beginning at evening." PNEUMA: "From the evening of the ninth day." Consensus: "On the ninth day of the month at evening" — EMET/LOGOS adjusted. The Hebrew *ba-erev* is "in the evening." LOGOS's "beginning at evening" adds a word for clarity; PNEUMA's "From the evening of" is paraphrastic. The simpler "at evening" is preferred as more formally equivalent.

**V. 34 — "Feast of Booths" for *chag ha-sukkot*.** All three agents: EMET and LOGOS use "Feast of Booths." PNEUMA: "Festival of Tabernacles." Consensus: "Feast of Booths" — EMET/LOGOS. "Booths" (*sukkot* = temporary shelters) is the more literal and established rendering; "Tabernacles" is the traditional Latin-derived term (used in KJV tradition) but is less transparent. "Feast of Booths" is preferred.

**V. 36 — "Solemn assembly" for *atseret*.** EMET: "a solemn assembly [*atseret*]." LOGOS: "a solemn assembly." PNEUMA: "the closing assembly." Consensus: "a solemn assembly" — EMET/LOGOS. The term *atseret* (from *atsar*, "to hold back/close/assemble") denotes the formal concluding assembly of the festival. LOGOS/ESV's "solemn assembly" is the established rendering; PNEUMA's "closing assembly" is accurate but less standard. The consensus distinguishes *atseret* ("solemn assembly") from *miqra-qodesh* ("holy convocation").

**V. 37 — "Each on its proper day."** EMET: "each day's matter in its day [*devar yom be-yomo*]." LOGOS: "each on its proper day." PNEUMA: "each on its proper day." Consensus: "each on its proper day" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "each day's matter in its day" is a more wooden rendering of the Hebrew idiom *devar yom be-yomo*; LOGOS captures the meaning more idiomatically.

**V. 38 — "Besides... besides... besides... besides."** EMET and LOGOS preserve the fourfold *mi-levad* ("besides/apart from") formula. PNEUMA uses "in addition to... and in addition to." Consensus: "besides... and besides... and besides... and besides" — preserving the fourfold anaphora of the MT. LOGOS's "besides" is correct.

**V. 39 — "Solemn rest" vs. "rest" for *shabbaton* (vv. 39).** EMET: "a rest [*shabbaton*]." LOGOS: "a solemn rest." PNEUMA: "a day of rest." Consensus: "a solemn rest" — LOGOS. In v. 39 the term is *shabbaton*, the same term used in v. 24 for the Feast of Trumpets, where "solemn rest" was adopted. The consistency of *shabbaton* = "solemn rest" is maintained throughout (vv. 24, 32, 39).

**V. 39 — "When you have gathered in the produce of the land."** EMET: "when you gather in the produce [*tevu'at*] of the land." LOGOS: "when you have gathered in the produce of the land." PNEUMA: "after you have gathered the crops of the land." Consensus: "when you have gathered in the produce of the land" — LOGOS. "Produce" (*tevu'at*) is more precise than PNEUMA's "crops" (though both are acceptable). The perfective "have gathered" captures the completion of the harvest that occasions the feast.

**V. 40 — "The fruit of splendid trees."** EMET: "the fruit of a majestic tree [*peri ets hadar*]." LOGOS: "the fruit of splendid trees." PNEUMA: "branches from splendid trees." Consensus: "the fruit of splendid trees" — LOGOS. The MT has *peri ets hadar* ("fruit of a splendid/glorious tree"). EMET's "majestic" is also good; LOGOS's "splendid" is more established (following ESV). PNEUMA substitutes "branches" for "fruit" (*peri*) — inadmissible, as the Hebrew clearly specifies *peri* (fruit). The word "branches" occurs in PNEUMA because of the following items (palm branches, leafy boughs), but *peri* must be rendered "fruit" here.

**V. 40 — "Branches of palm trees."** EMET: "fronds of palm trees [*kappot temarim*]." LOGOS: "branches of palm trees." PNEUMA: "palm fronds." Consensus: "branches of palm trees" — LOGOS. *Kappot* means "fronds/branches"; "branches of palm trees" is the standard formal rendering (ESV). "Palm fronds" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but shorter; LOGOS is preferred for parallelism with the following clauses.

**V. 40 — "Boughs of leafy trees."** EMET: "boughs of leafy trees [*anaf ets avot*]." LOGOS: "boughs of leafy trees." PNEUMA: "leafy boughs." Consensus: "boughs of leafy trees" — LOGOS/EMET. The MT has *anaf ets avot* ("branches of dense/thick trees"); LOGOS/EMET preserve both the branch-term and the tree-term. PNEUMA's "leafy boughs" is a compression that loses the tree reference.

**V. 40 — "Willows of the brook."** All three: "willows of the brook [*arvei-nachal*]" — agreement. Consensus: "willows of the brook."

**V. 41 — "You shall celebrate it as a feast."** EMET: "you shall keep it as a feast [*chag la-YHWH*]." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "celebrate it as a feast to the LORD." Consensus: "you shall celebrate it as a feast to the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The verb *chagag* ("to keep/celebrate a pilgrimage feast") is well rendered "celebrate." EMET's "keep" is accurate but "celebrate" better captures the joyful character of *chagag*.

**V. 42 — "All native Israelites."** EMET: "every native [*kol-ha-ezrach*] in Israel." LOGOS: "All native Israelites." PNEUMA: "all native-born Israelites." Consensus: "all native Israelites" — LOGOS. *Ha-ezrach* = "the native, one born in the land" — a technical term for the native Israelite as distinct from the *ger* (sojourner). Both LOGOS and PNEUMA are accurate; "native Israelites" is cleaner.

**V. 43 — "I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths."** EMET: "I caused the sons of Israel to dwell [*hoshavti*] in booths." LOGOS: "I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths." PNEUMA: "I made the Israelites live in temporary shelters." Consensus: "I made the sons of Israel dwell in booths" — LOGOS. The Hiphil *hoshavti* ("I caused to dwell/I settled") is rendered "made... dwell." "Booths" is preserved (PNEUMA's "temporary shelters" is an interpretive gloss on *sukkot* rather than a translation). "Sons of Israel" maintained per project convention.

**V. 44 — "Thus Moses declared."** EMET: "And Moses declared [*va-yedabber Moshe*]." LOGOS: "Thus Moses declared." PNEUMA: "So Moses announced." Consensus: "Thus Moses declared" — LOGOS. This closing verse is the only place in Leviticus where Moses is the subject of *dibber* rather than YHWH. "Declared" captures the formal proclamatory sense. PNEUMA's "announced" is weaker; EMET's "And Moses declared" is also good, but LOGOS's "Thus" signals the summary function of the verse more clearly.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On *isheh* ("fire-offering" vs. "food offering"):** EMET consistently renders *isheh* as "fire-offering," reflecting the traditional derivation from *esh* ("fire"). LOGOS and PNEUMA use "food offering," following modern scholarship (ESV, NASB) that derives the term from a Ugaritic cognate meaning "food/gift." The consensus follows LOGOS/PNEUMA's "food offering" throughout. This is not a trivial disagreement: the traditional etymology connects every festival sacrifice to the altar fire; the modern reading frames them as provisions or tributes to the LORD. The matter is noted in the disagreement log but the consensus adopts the modern formal-equivalent convention.

**On *kol-melakhah* vs. *melaket avodah*:** The chapter uses two distinct work-prohibition formulas: (a) *kol-melakhah* ("all work") — used for the weekly Sabbath (v. 3) and the Day of Atonement (v. 28), where the prohibition is total; and (b) *kol-melaket avodah* ("all laborious work") — used for each festival day, where a lesser category of servile labor is prohibited while other activities (cooking, carrying) may be permitted. The consensus carefully preserves this distinction throughout: "you shall do no work" for (a), "you shall do no laborious work" for (b). This distinction is systematically collapsed by LOGOS (which uses "ordinary work" for both categories) and by PNEUMA (which uses "regular work" throughout), making it a significant disagreement recorded in the log.

**On the feminine pronouns in vv. 29–30:** The Hebrew *ha-nefesh* ("the person/soul") is grammatically feminine, producing *me-ammeiha* ("from her people") in v. 29 and *mi-qerev ammah* ("from among her people") in v. 30. These feminine pronouns refer to any person — the legal subject is generic. EMET preserves the feminine Hebrew form; LOGOS normalizes to masculine ("his people"); PNEUMA uses modern English "their people." The consensus preserves "her people" as the formally exact rendering of the Hebrew, noting that the grammar reflects the feminine noun *nefesh* rather than a gender specification about the legal subject.

**On *zikron teru'ah* (v. 24):** "A memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" — the phrase *zikron teru'ah* joins two nouns: *zikron* ("memorial/remembrance") and *teru'ah* ("trumpet blast/shout/alarm"). The LXX renders *mnēmosunon salpingōn* ("a memorial of trumpets"), which emphasizes the plurality of blasts. The Numbers 29:1 parallel calls the day *yom teru'ah* ("a day of trumpet blast"). The rendering "memorial proclaimed by a trumpet blast" (LOGOS) captures both dimensions without adding "shofar" (the instrument is not named in the text).

**On *atseret* (v. 36):** The term *atseret* ("solemn assembly") is the closing assembly of Tabernacles. The LXX renders *exodion* ("a closing/departure [feast]"), emphasizing the formal departure from the festival season. The consensus uses "solemn assembly" (LOGOS/ESV) to distinguish *atseret* from the regular *miqra-qodesh* ("holy convocation") that appears at the opening day of each festival. Both terms involve assembly and cessation of work, but *atseret* specifically marks a formal conclusion.

**On the gleaning law in v. 22:** The insertion of the gleaning law (cf. Lev 19:9–10) within the festival calendar at v. 22 is characteristic of the Holiness Code's move: the celebration of the harvest festivals cannot be separated from care for those who have no harvest. The law is placed between Shavuot (v. 21) and the Feast of Trumpets (v. 23) — a deliberate theological placement indicating that the abundance celebrated at the harvest feast carries an obligation to the poor and the sojourner. PNEUMA's notes articulate this connection well, but for the consensus text, the verse is rendered without the dynamic commentary inserted into the translation.

**On v. 44 as the formal promulgation:** The closing verse uses *va-yedabber Moshe* — Moses as the active subject of *dibber* — a unique construction in Leviticus where YHWH is otherwise always the speaker. This marks the formal handing-over of the sacred calendar to the covenant community: YHWH appoints the times; Moses declares them; Israel proclaims them. The three-stage transmission is the theological architecture of the whole chapter.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 23:1–44. All 44 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_023.md`.*
